perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means any and all information disclosed by Owner that is identified as "confidential" or "proprietary," either by legend on written or electronically stored material, or in advance if disclosed verbally. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, research and development, know-how, inventions, trade secrets, software, and market analysis, research, strategies, projections and forecasts. Confidential Information also includes, without limitation, information disclosed by Owner with permission from a third party, and combinations of or with publicly known information where the nature of the combination is not publicly known.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Confidential Information" means information disclosed by Owner that the Recipient deems confidential. Confidential Information includes, without limitation, research and development, know-how, inventions, trade secrets, software, and market analysis, research, strategies, projections and forecasts. Confidential Information also includes, without limitation, information disclosed by Owner with permission from a third party, and combinations of or with publicly known information where the nature of the combination is not publicly known.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition stated that information must be identified as confidential 'by legend on written or electronically stored material, or in advance if disclosed verbally' by the Owner to be considered confidential. The modified text removes the Owner's ability to designate the information as such, instead giving sole discretion to the Recipient. This creates an in-text contradiction, as Section 2, Covenant Not to Disclose, refers to 'Owner's Confidential Information', implying the Owner has a defined way to identify it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2. Covenant Not to Disclose. Recipient shall not use Owner's Confidential Information except for purposes of evaluating, maintaining and furthering a business relationship with Owner. Recipient shall maintain in confidence the Confidential Information received from Owner and shall not directly or indirectly disclose such information to any person or entity except Recipient's employees and consultants, and then only on a need-to-know basis. Recipient shall ensure that such employees and consultants are bound by a written agreement to protect the received Confidential Information from unauthorized use and disclosure. Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own most highly confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care. Recipient shall not make or distribute any more copies or summaries of Owner's Confidential Information than are necessary to evaluate, maintain and further a business relationship between the Parties, and shall ensure that all such copies or summaries are marked as confidential and the property of Owner.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Owner to designate what is considered confidential information. The changed text gives that right to the Recipient. Section 2, Covenant Not to Disclose, refers to 'Owner's Confidential Information' and puts obligations on the Recipient. However, with the changed text, the Recipient decides what is confidential, undermining the Owner's rights, and contradicting Section 2.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1.1 Exceptions. Confidential Information does not include information which: (a) was publicly known at the time of Owner's communication thereof to Recipient, or which subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of Recipient; (b) was in the possession of Recipient prior to its being communicated to Recipient by Owner; (c) becomes available to Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a source other than Owner, provided that such source is not bound by any obligation of confidentiality to Owner with respect to such information; or (d) was independently developed by Recipient without reference to the Confidential Information communicated by Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1.1 Exceptions. Confidential Information does not include information which: (a) was publicly known at the time of Owner's communication thereof to Recipient, or which subsequently becomes publicly known through no fault of Recipient; (b) was in the possession of Recipient prior to its being communicated to Recipient by Owner; (c) becomes available to Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a source other than Owner; or (d) was independently developed by Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause (c) included the caveat 'provided that such source is not bound by any obligation of confidentiality to Owner with respect to such information'. Its removal creates a contradiction with Section 2, which obligates the Recipient to protect Owner's Confidential Information. Now, even if the information came from a source violating their confidentiality agreement with the Owner, it's no longer considered confidential under 1.1.1, weakening the obligations in Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2. Covenant Not to Disclose. Recipient shall not use Owner's Confidential Information except for purposes of evaluating, maintaining and furthering a business relationship with Owner. Recipient shall maintain in confidence the Confidential Information received from Owner and shall not directly or indirectly disclose such information to any person or entity except Recipient's employees and consultants, and then only on a need-to-know basis. Recipient shall ensure that such employees and consultants are bound by a written agreement to protect the received Confidential Information from unauthorized use and disclosure. Recipient shall protect Owner's Confidential Information from disclosure to others using the same degree of care that it uses to protect its own most highly confidential information, but in no event less than a reasonable standard of care. Recipient shall not make or distribute any more copies or summaries of Owner's Confidential Information than are necessary to evaluate, maintain and further a business relationship between the Parties, and shall ensure that all such copies or summaries are marked as confidential and the property of Owner.
perturbation.justification: The removal of the phrase 'provided that such source is not bound by any obligation of confidentiality to Owner with respect to such information' from clause 1.1.1(c) creates a direct contradiction with Section 2. Section 2 mandates that the Recipient protect Owner's Confidential Information. However, the changed text in 1.1.1(c) now implies that if the information becomes available to the Recipient from any source other than the Owner, regardless of whether that source is violating a confidentiality agreement with the Owner, it is no longer considered Confidential Information. This directly undermines the Recipient's obligation to protect the Owner's Confidential Information as outlined in Section 2, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall ensure that such employees and consultants are bound by a written agreement to protect the received Confidential Information from unauthorized use and disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall encourage that such employees and consultants are bound by an agreement to protect the received Confidential Information from unauthorized use and disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stated that the recipient must ensure that all employees and consultants are bound by a written agreement to protect the received Confidential Information from unauthorized use and disclosure. The removal of 'written' creates ambiguity, and changing 'ensure' to 'encourage' weakens the binding nature of the confidentiality agreement. This now contradicts the strong confidentiality provisions outlined in Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 7. Specific Performance. The Parties acknowledge that Confidential Information is unique and valuable, and that Owner will have no adequate remedy at law if Recipient does not comply with its obligations under this Agreement. Therefore, Owner shall have the right, in addition to any other rights it may have, to obtain in any court of competent jurisdiction temporary, preliminary and permanent injunctive relief to restrain any breach, threatened breach, or otherwise to specifically enforce any obligations of Recipient if Recipient fails to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the requirement for a 'written agreement' to merely 'an agreement' and changes 'ensure' to 'encourage'. This directly contradicts Section 7, which emphasizes the importance of protecting confidential information and provides specific performance remedies for breaches of the agreement. The original text ensured a strong and enforceable agreement while the changed text introduces ambiguity and reduces enforceability, making it difficult to enforce the obligations outlined in Section 7. The explanation states that this creates ambiguity, and changing 'ensure' to 'encourage' weakens the binding nature of the confidentiality agreement. This now contradicts the strong confidentiality provisions outlined in Section 2.

perturbation.original_text: Section 3. No Obligation Of Disclosure. Neither Party shall have any obligation to disclose its Confidential Information to the other. Either Party may, at any time, cease providing its Confidential Information to the other Party, and may require the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed by written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3. No Obligation Of Disclosure. Neither Party shall have any obligation to disclose its Confidential Information to the other. Either Party may, at any time, cease providing its Confidential Information to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The deletion of the clause 'and may require the return of Confidential Information previously disclosed by written notice' creates a contradiction with Section 6. Section 6 states that 'Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form...' Without the ability to formally request the information's return as specified within the original Section 3, enforcing the termination terms in Section 6 becomes ambiguous and difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6. Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. All obligations of confidentiality shall survive such termination. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.justification: The original text in Section 3 allowed a party to require the return of confidential information by written notice. The changed text removes this ability. Section 6 mandates the return of confidential information upon termination. Without the explicit right to request the return as initially stated in Section 3, it is now unclear how Section 6 can be effectively enforced because there's no established mechanism in Section 3 to initiate the return process outside of termination, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid, such invalidity shall not render invalid the remainder of this Agreement or the remainder of which such invalid provision is a part. If any provision of this Agreement is so broad as to be held unenforceable, such provision shall be interpreted to be only so broad as is enforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid, such invalidity shall render the agreement invalid, as it can't be enforced.
perturbation.explanation: The original text had severability and enforceability clauses, allowing parts of the agreement to remain valid even if others are deemed invalid or unenforceable. By replacing it with a statement that invalidity renders the entire agreement void, the original agreement is effectively nullified. This directly contradicts Section 8.4, which states 'This Agreement constitutes and embodies the entire agreement and understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous written, electronic or oral communications, agreements or understandings between the parties with respect thereto.' Now, an invalid provision destroys the entire agreement, affecting the entire scope.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.4 Entire Agreement; Amendments. This Agreement constitutes and embodies the entire agreement and understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous written, electronic or oral communications, agreements or understandings between the parties with respect thereto. This Agreement may not be modified or amended except by a written instrument executed by the parties. Page 13 of 34
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts section 8.4. The original text had a severability clause which allowed for the agreement to still be valid. The changed text removes this and states the agreement is invalid, directly contradicting the entire agreement clause, which makes the original agreement void. Therefore, there is a strong in-text contradiction. The change adheres to the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction: Omissions occur when essential language is removed or altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement given by either party to the other party shall be deemed to be properly given if given in writing and delivered (i) by nationally recognized private courier (e.g., Federal Express) or (ii) by mail (return receipt requested), properly addressed and stamped with the required postage, to the recipient at the address identified in its signature block to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement given by either party to the other party shall be deemed to be properly given if delivered to the recipient at the address identified in its signature block to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirements for written notice and specific delivery methods (courier or certified mail) creates ambiguity. This contradicts Section 6, Termination, where it mentions the requirement of a written notice. Now, without specific requirements to ensure the notice, a dispute will arise whether the notice has been given.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6. Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. All obligations of confidentiality shall survive such termination. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall promptly return all of Owner's Confidential Information provided to it in tangible form, together with any and all copies and/or summaries, and shall destroy all of Owner's Confidential Information that is electronically stored; provided, however, that each Party's legal department may retain one copy of the Confidential Information in its file solely for the purpose of identifying information designated as "Confidential Information."
perturbation.justification: The original text specified methods (courier/mail) to deliver notice. The changed text removes those methods. The contradicted text (Section 6) requires 'written notice'. Removing the specification of how to give notice, especially the requirement of it being in writing, creates an ambiguity about how written notice can be properly given, thus contradicting Section 6, which necessitates it.
